stem: The cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is generated by \( 1 \) under addition modulo [[n]]. What is the order of the element \( [[k]] \) in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), i.e., the smallest positive integer \( d \) with \( d\cdot[[k]] \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \)?
solution: In a cyclic group of order \( n \) generated by \( 1 \), the order of the element \( k \) is \( \frac{n}{\gcd(n,k)} \). Here that is \( \frac{[[n]]}{\gcd([[n]],[[k]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \gcd([[n]],[[k]]) \) itself, not \( n \) divided by it.
This assumes every element generates the whole group, which is only true for generators.
This confuses the element's value with its order.
This subtracts the element from the modulus for no valid reason.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: How many of the [[n]] elements of the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) are generators of the group (that is, have order exactly [[n]])?
solution: The number of generators of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) is Euler's totient \( \varphi(n) \). Here \( \varphi([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats every non-identity element as a generator, which only holds when [[n]] is prime.
This counts the whole group, including the identity, none of which need be generators.
This is the number of SUBGROUPS of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) (one per divisor), not the number of generators.
This arbitrarily halves the group size instead of applying \( \varphi \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[n]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[n]]) \).

stem: The cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) has order \( [[n]] = [[d]]\cdot[[m]] \), so [[d]] is a divisor of \( [[n]] \). How many elements of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) have order exactly [[d]]?
solution: For each divisor [[d]] of \( n = [[n]] \), the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) has a unique subgroup of order [[d]], and the number of elements of order exactly [[d]] equals \( \varphi([[d]]) \). Here \( \varphi([[d]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats every nonzero element of the order-[[d]] subgroup as having order [[d]], which is only true when [[d]] is prime.
This restates [[d]] itself instead of applying \( \varphi \).
This applies \( \varphi \) to the wrong factor [[m]] instead of [[d]].
This restates the cofactor [[m]] instead of computing \( \varphi([[d]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[d]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \varphi([[d]]) \).

stem: Since \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) = 1 \), the external direct product \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is itself cyclic, generated by \( (1,1) \). What is the order of this cyclic group (equivalently, the order of the generator \( (1,1) \))?
solution: Since \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]])=1 \), the order of \( (1,1) \) is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m]],[[n]]) = [[m]]\cdot[[n]] = [[answer]] \), which is also \( |\mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]}\times\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]}| \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two factor sizes instead of multiplying (taking their lcm).
This uses only the first factor's size, ignoring the second.
This uses only the second factor's size, ignoring the first.
The gcd of coprime numbers is 1, not the order of the product group.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: In the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), how many elements \( x \) satisfy \( [[d]]\cdot x \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \)?
solution: The solutions to \( [[d]] x \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \) form the subgroup of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) of order \( \gcd([[n]],[[d]]) \). Here \( \gcd([[n]],[[d]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes every element of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is a solution, but most are not.
This restates [[d]] itself rather than computing the gcd.
This is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[n]],[[d]]) \), which is unrelated to the solution count.
This divides [[n]] by [[d]] directly, which need not even be an integer.
This is off by one from \( \gcd([[n]],[[d]]) \).
This is off by one from \( \gcd([[n]],[[d]]) \).
